title: Expansion of presidential power
description: Questions on the expansion of presidential power.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“That unity is conducive to energy will not be disputed. Decision, activity, secrecy, and despatch will generally characterize the proceedings of one man in a much more eminent degree than the proceedings of any greater number; and in proportion as the number is increased, these qualities will be diminished . . .”

>-Alexander Hamilton, Federalist No. 70, “The Executive Department Further Considered,” 1788 

**Which of the following scenarios would be considered an example of how unity is conducive to energy in the executive branch, as defined by the author of the passage? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: John F. Kennedy’s secret negotiations ending the Cuban Missile Crisis
Bill Clinton’s “open White House,” with many aides who played a role in shaping policy
George W. Bush’s 2002 State of the Union address, in which he asked the Congress to enact new safeguards for pension plans
Lyndon Johnson’s decision not to run for reelection in 1968

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Energy in the Executive is a leading character in the definition of good government. It is essential to the protection of the community against foreign attacks; it is not less essential to the steady administration of the laws; to the protection of property against those irregular and high-handed combinations which sometimes interrupt the ordinary course of justice; to the security of liberty against the enterprises and assaults of ambition, of faction, and of anarchy . . .” 

>-Alexander Hamilton, Federalist No. 70, “The Executive Department Further Considered,” 1788 

**“Energy,” as used by Hamilton in Federalist No. 70, is best defined as which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The capacity to act quickly and decisively
The youthful vigor necessary to withstand the pressure of the job
The charm necessary to influence Congress to enact a policy agenda
The work ethic to run the government effectively

assessment_items.question_content:
>“Energy in the Executive is a leading character in the definition of good government. It is essential to the protection of the community against foreign attacks; it is not less essential to the steady administration of the laws; to the protection of property against those irregular and high-handed combinations which sometimes interrupt the ordinary course of justice; to the security of liberty against the enterprises and assaults of ambition, of faction, and of anarchy . . .” 

>-Alexander Hamilton, Federalist No. 70, “The Executive Department Further Considered,” 1788 

**Which of the following is a consequence of the ideas set forth by the author of this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The establishment of the president as Commander in Chief of the military
The requirement that the president be at least 35 years old
The requirement that the president will periodically give information to Congress on the state of the Union
The Congressional power to impeach a president for high crimes and misdemeanors

assessment_items.question_content:
![A political cartoon, in which a ghostly figure of George Washington labeled 'Anti-Third Term Principle points an accusing finger at Teddy Roosevelt, who is cowering and holding a piece of paper labeled "My Policies, My Platform, My Issues."](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4717c2280295fc23899fa32b30a3194d.jpg)
"Anti-Third Term Principle," by Clifford K. Berryman, 1912. The cartoon depicts Theodore Roosevelt, left, and George Washington, right. Source: [National Archives](https://www.archives.gov/historical-docs/todays-doc/?dod-date=227)

**Which of the following best describes the message in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Presidential power should be restrained by term-of-office limits
Presidents after George Washington should strive to emulate his foreign policy
Presidents should only seek a third term if their policies and platforms require it
Washington was wrong to step down after only two terms in office

assessment_items.question_content:
![A political cartoon, in which a ghostly figure of George Washington labeled 'Anti-Third Term Principle points an accusing finger at Teddy Roosevelt, who is cowering and holding a piece of paper labeled "My Policies, My Platform, My Issues."](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4717c2280295fc23899fa32b30a3194d.jpg)
"Anti-Third Term Principle," by Clifford K. Berryman, 1912. The cartoon depicts Theodore Roosevelt, left, and George Washington, right. Source: [National Archives](https://www.archives.gov/historical-docs/todays-doc/?dod-date=227)

**Which of the following Constitutional Amendments is most relevant to the topic of the cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 3]]

choices.content: The Twenty-second Amendment
The Fourteenth Amendment
The Nineteenth Amendment
The Twenty-first Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following scenarios would most likely be considered a violation of the Twenty-second Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A sitting president runs for a third term in office after overwhelming support from the American public
A sitting president commits troops to a military conflict without a declaration of war from Congress
A member of Congress runs for a sixth term in office after receiving support from her constituents
Congress blocks an executive order after passing a bill declaring all executive orders null and void

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the passage below to answer the following question.

>“Both Lincoln and Roosevelt undertook acts they knew to be beyond the Constitution. Both did so in times of transcendent crisis when the life of the nation seemed truly at stake. Both acted, knowingly or not, on Locke’s doctrine of emergency prerogative, trusting that Congress would eventually approve their actions.” 

>-Arthur Schlesinger, *The Imperial Presidency,* 1973

**Which of the following statements best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Presidents have enhanced their constitutional powers beyond what is expressed in the Constitution in order to deal with major crises
Presidents have always respected the separation of powers and have only taken more power when Congress approved
Presidents have enhanced the power of the presidency through the creation of new executive agencies
When presidents have attempted to take more power, Congress has constrained them

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the passage below to answer the following question.

>“Both Lincoln and Roosevelt undertook acts they knew to be beyond the Constitution. Both did so in times of transcendent crisis when the life of the nation seemed truly at stake. Both acted, knowingly or not, on Locke’s doctrine of emergency prerogative, trusting that Congress would eventually approve their actions.” 

>-Arthur Schlesinger, *The Imperial Presidency,* 1973

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Presidential power has increased in the face of growing federal challenges
Presidential power has decreased as Congress has become more powerful
Presidential power has decreased as executive agencies have taken on more responsibilities
Presidential power has increased as Congress has passed resolutions to expand executive warmaking powers

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the passage below to answer the following question. 

>“That unity is conducive to energy will not be disputed. Decision, activity, secrecy, and despatch will generally characterize the proceedings of one man in a much more eminent degree than the proceedings of any greater number; and in proportion as the number is increased, these qualities will be diminished . . .”

>-Alexander Hamilton, Federalist No. 70, “The Executive Department Further Considered,” 1788

**Which of the following statements best summarizes Hamilton’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: One person should run the executive branch because they will be able to act swiftly, decisively, and discreetly, which is necessary for the presidency
Three people should run the executive branch because it is too much power for one person to hold
Three people should run the executive branch because its power to negotiate treaties with other countries should be distributed to multiple people
One person should run the executive branch because it will be too complicated for Congress if they have to negotiate with multiple people

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the passage below to answer the following question.

>“The mystique of inside information—”if you only knew what we know”—was a most effective way to defend the national-security monopoly and prevent democratic control of foreign policy . . . 

>"The power to leak meant the power to tell the people what it served the government’s purpose that they should know . . . 

>"The power to withhold and the power to leak led on inexorably to the power to lie. The secrecy system instilled in the executive branch the idea that foreign policy was no one’s business save its own, and the uncontrolled secrecy made it easy for lying to become routine.”

>-Arthur Schlesinger, *The Imperial Presidency*, 1973

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The executive branch has too much power in implementing foreign policy
The executive branch should not control foreign policy or national security
Secrecy is necessary for effective government action abroad
Government leaks ensure that corruption in the executive branch is exposed

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the passage below to answer the following question.

>“The mystique of inside information—”if you only knew what we know”—was a most effective way to defend the national-security monopoly and prevent democratic control of foreign policy . . . 

>The power to leak meant the power to tell the people what it served the government’s purpose that they should know . . . 

>The power to withhold and the power to leak led on inexorably to the power to lie. The secrecy system instilled in the executive branch the idea that foreign policy was no one’s business save its own, and the uncontrolled secrecy made it easy for lying to become routine.”

>-Arthur Schlesinger, *The Imperial Presidency*, 1973


**Which of the following governmental policies would the author most likely support?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The War Powers Resolution of 1973, which aimed to give Congress more say in whether US troops were deployed abroad
The Patriot Act of 2001, which expanded government surveillance measures in order to combat terrorism
The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution of 1964, which authorized the president to do whatever was necessary to aid US allies in Southeast Asia
The Obama administration’s 2016 decision not to enforce immigration laws mandating the deportation of undocumented young people

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the passage below to answer the following question.

>“The mystique of inside information—”if you only knew what we know”—was a most effective way to defend the national-security monopoly and prevent democratic control of foreign policy . . . 

>The power to leak meant the power to tell the people what it served the government’s purpose that they should know . . . 

>The power to withhold and the power to leak led on inexorably to the power to lie. The secrecy system instilled in the executive branch the idea that foreign policy was no one’s business save its own, and the uncontrolled secrecy made it easy for lying to become routine.”

>-Arthur Schlesinger, *The Imperial Presidency*, 1973

**Which of the following scenarios provides the best justification for an expansive interpretation of presidential power?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The rapid response necessary after a threat of nuclear war
The Congressional deadlock resulting from hyper-partisanship
The inconsistency of judicial interpretations of the Constitution
The popularity of the president compared to the popularity of Congress

assessment_items.question_content:
After U.S. intelligence agencies confirm that a foreign government interfered in recent federal elections, the president responds by announcing new sanctions on foreign nations, ordering the removal of several diplomats, and directing federal cybersecurity agencies to expand their defensive actions. These steps are taken without waiting for congressional authorization.

**What best explains how the president’s response demonstrates an expansive interpretation of executive power?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The president is exercising informal authority to take independent action in foreign policy.
The president is enforcing laws passed by Congress to regulate foreign commerce.
The president is using the commander-in-chief role to deploy military personnel abroad.
The president is carrying out a Supreme Court order to protect national security.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes a difference between a president with a limited view of executive power and a president with a more expansive view?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A limited-view president is more likely to defer to Congress on domestic policy.
A limited-view president won’t participate in foreign policy decisions.
An expansive-view president is less likely to use the veto power.
An expansive-view president will ignore Supreme Court rulings that limit executive action.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which best explains a major difference between the presidency today and the presidency as envisioned by the delegates at the Constitutional Convention?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Modern presidents are expected to lead political parties more actively than originally intended.
Modern presidents face fewer checks on power than originally intended.
Modern presidents are required to have more prior political experience than originally intended.
Modern presidents have less control over foreign policy than the Constitution suggests they should.
